questions.text: I routinely track my personal progress and set benchmarks for my own improvement.
I would sacrifice a personal passion if pursuing it took resources away from people who depend on me.
I experience emotional triggers as a kind of switch that flips instantly.
I believe that the felt weight of suffering is the moral experience that most urgently calls me to act.
I find that I'm rarely caught off guard by the intensity of my own emotional responses.
I tend not to register when someone is being deliberately evasive.
I believe that the moral urgency of suffering is a signal I have learned to take seriously, and I would not want to lose that capacity.
I prefer to let someone else set the direction before I contribute.
I pick up on the way people position themselves physically relative to those they trust.
I notice that I frequently steer conversations toward action.
I trust that the things that truly matter will stay in my mind without a list.
I feel that taking charge is a natural extension of caring about results.
I make sure to have the right tools and materials ready before starting any task.
I can tell from someone's greeting whether they are having a good or bad day.
I believe that patience and persistence are more important than talent in achieving mastery.
I find that my default setting is to consider what others need before what I want.
I hold the view that maintaining rational focus during emotionally intense events is a deliberate practice that yields better judgment with each iteration.
I experience creative inspiration as an almost feverish state.
